name: Behavioral Science
description: An interdisciplinary exploration of the biological, psychological, and social forces that shape human and animal behavior, with applications to health, economics, and society.

modules.title: Introduction to Behavioral Science
modules.objectives: Define behavioral science as an interdisciplinary field, identify its major subfields, and distinguish it from pure psychology or biology.
modules.assignment: Write a 300-word essay defining behavioral science and explaining how it differs from traditional psychology, sociology, and biology. Provide one example of a behavioral science application in each of these three domains.

modules.title: Evolutionary Psychology
modules.objectives: Apply natural selection principles to explain evolved psychological mechanisms, evaluate the evolutionary basis of altruism, mate selection, and aggression.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Biological Bases of Behavior
modules.assignment: Choose one human behavior (such as fear of snakes, jealousy, or cooperation) and write a 350-word evolutionary explanation. Include a discussion of whether the adaptive function still applies in modern environments.

modules.title: Behavioral Genetics
modules.objectives: Explain heritability, distinguish between twin and adoption study methods, and evaluate the nature-nurture interaction in traits like intelligence and personality.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 3.1 — Genetics and Behavior
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Behavioral Genetics: Nature vs. Nurture
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Biology of Behavior Practice
modules.assignment: Analyze the Minnesota Twin Study findings. Write a 350-word evaluation of what twin research tells us about the heritability of intelligence and personality, and identify two limitations of the twin study design.

modules.title: Classical Conditioning
modules.objectives: Describe the principles of Pavlovian conditioning, explain acquisition, extinction, generalization, and discrimination, and apply conditioning to explain phobias and taste aversions.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Pavlov's Dog and Classical Conditioning
modules.assignment: Identify a personal example of classical conditioning in your own life (e.g., a food aversion or emotional response to a song). Write a 300-word analysis labeling the UCS, UCR, CS, and CR, and explain how extinction could occur.

modules.title: Operant Conditioning
modules.objectives: Distinguish positive and negative reinforcement from punishment, compare reinforcement schedules, explain shaping and chaining, and evaluate Skinner's contributions.
modules.assignment: Design a behavior shaping program to teach a new skill to an animal or a young child using successive approximations. Write a 350-word step-by-step plan specifying the reinforcement type, schedule, and each successive step.

modules.title: Cognitive-Behavioral Perspectives
modules.objectives: Explain how cognitive processes mediate behavioral responses, describe cognitive distortions, and explain the ABC model used in cognitive-behavioral therapy.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — What is Cognitive Behavioral Therapy?
modules.assignment: Apply Ellis's ABC model to a personal situation where an event led to an unhelpful emotional reaction. Write a 300-word analysis identifying the Activating event (A), Belief (B), and Consequence (C), then propose a rational alternative belief.

modules.title: Social Learning Theory
modules.objectives: Describe Bandura's social learning theory, explain the role of modeling, vicarious reinforcement, and self-efficacy in behavior acquisition and maintenance.
modules.assignment: Identify a role model who has significantly influenced your behavior or aspirations. Write a 350-word analysis applying Bandura's four components of observational learning (attention, retention, reproduction, motivation) to explain this influence.

modules.title: Behavior Modification
modules.objectives: Apply reinforcement, punishment, token economies, and self-management strategies to design behavior change programs in educational and clinical settings.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 6.2 — Applied Behavior Principles
modules.assignment: Design a token economy system for a classroom of 10-year-olds to increase study behavior and reduce off-task behavior. Write a 400-word plan specifying target behaviors, token earning rules, exchange rates, and backup reinforcers.

modules.title: Behavioral Economics
modules.objectives: Explain how psychological biases (loss aversion, anchoring, status quo bias) influence economic decision-making, and describe nudge theory applications.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Behavioral Economics: The Hidden Forces
modules.assignment: Analyze a real-world public policy that uses behavioral economics principles (such as organ donation opt-out, retirement savings defaults, or calorie labeling). Write a 400-word evaluation of its effectiveness and ethical implications.

modules.title: Organizational Behavior
modules.objectives: Describe how psychological principles apply to workplace motivation, leadership styles, group dynamics, and organizational culture.
modules.assignment: Interview an adult about their workplace motivation. Using Herzberg's two-factor theory, identify which hygiene factors and motivators are present or absent in their job. Write a 400-word analysis with recommendations for improving workplace satisfaction.

modules.title: Consumer Behavior
modules.objectives: Explain how attitudes, social influence, cognitive biases, and emotional appeals shape consumer purchasing decisions and brand loyalty.
modules.assignment: Analyze a recent purchase you or your family made. Write a 400-word analysis identifying at least three psychological factors (such as scarcity, social proof, anchoring, or framing) that influenced the decision.

modules.title: Environmental Influences on Behavior
modules.objectives: Describe how physical environments (crowding, noise, temperature, green space) and social environments influence stress, cognition, aggression, and prosocial behavior.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 14 — Stress, Health & Coping
modules.assignment: Observe and document your own environment for one day (home, school, neighborhood). Write a 400-word analysis identifying how at least three environmental factors influenced your mood, concentration, or social behavior during that day.

modules.title: Behavioral Health & Public Health
modules.objectives: Explain the role of behavior change in preventing disease and promoting health, describe the Health Belief Model and Transtheoretical Model, and evaluate public health campaign strategies.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 14 — Stress, Lifestyle & Health
modules.assignment: Apply the Transtheoretical Model (stages of change) to a personal health behavior you want to modify (such as sleep, exercise, or diet). Write a 400-word plan identifying your current stage and specific behavioral strategies for each subsequent stage.

modules.title: Applied Behavior Analysis
modules.objectives: Define Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA), explain functional behavior assessment, and evaluate the use of ABA in autism treatment, education, and organizational settings.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Introduction to Applied Behavior Analysis
modules.assignment: Research the use of ABA in supporting individuals with autism spectrum disorder. Write a 450-word evidence-based analysis covering the key techniques used, documented benefits, ethical criticisms, and current best practices in the field.

quizQuestions.question: Behavioral science is best described as:
options: A subfield of medicine focused on drug therapy
An interdisciplinary field studying behavior through biology, psychology, and social science
The study of animal behavior only
quizQuestions.answer: An interdisciplinary field studying behavior through biology, psychology, and social science
quizQuestions.explanation: Behavioral science integrates biology, psychology, sociology, and other fields to understand human and animal behavior.

quizQuestions.question: Which discipline is NOT typically considered part of behavioral science?
quizQuestions.answer: Astronomy
quizQuestions.explanation: Astronomy studies celestial objects and has no direct connection to behavioral science.

quizQuestions.question: The primary goal of behavioral science research is to:
quizQuestions.answer: Predict and understand behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: Behavioral science seeks to understand, predict, and in applied contexts influence human and animal behavior.

quizQuestions.question: Which method is most central to behavioral science research?
quizQuestions.answer: Empirical observation and experimentation
quizQuestions.explanation: Behavioral science relies on systematic, empirical methods including observation, surveys, and controlled experiments.

quizQuestions.question: Applied behavioral science differs from basic behavioral science primarily in its:
quizQuestions.answer: Focus on solving real-world problems
quizQuestions.explanation: Applied behavioral science uses research findings to address practical issues in health, education, policy, and organizations.

quizQuestions.question: Evolutionary psychology proposes that psychological mechanisms evolved because they:
options: Increased reproductive success in ancestral environments
quizQuestions.answer: Increased reproductive success in ancestral environments
quizQuestions.explanation: Evolutionary psychology applies natural selection: psychological traits persisted because they improved survival and reproduction.

quizQuestions.question: The concept of inclusive fitness explains why organisms may sacrifice themselves for:
quizQuestions.answer: Genetically related relatives
quizQuestions.explanation: Hamilton's inclusive fitness theory explains altruism toward kin as benefiting shared genes.

quizQuestions.question: Sexual selection theory predicts that females will be more selective in mate choice because:
options: Their parental investment is typically greater
quizQuestions.answer: Their parental investment is typically greater
quizQuestions.explanation: Parental investment theory (Trivers) holds that the sex with greater investment (females in most species) is more selective in choosing mates.

quizQuestions.question: An evolutionary psychologist would argue that the fear of snakes is common because:
options: It is taught by parents universally
Snakes posed genuine survival threats in ancestral environments
The amygdala learned it from social experience
quizQuestions.answer: Snakes posed genuine survival threats in ancestral environments
quizQuestions.explanation: Preparedness theory (Seligman) suggests humans are biologically prepared to fear stimuli that threatened ancestors.

quizQuestions.question: A key criticism of evolutionary psychology is that its explanations are often:
quizQuestions.answer: Unfalsifiable post-hoc stories (just-so stories)
quizQuestions.explanation: Critics argue that evolutionary explanations are often constructed after the fact and difficult to test empirically.

quizQuestions.question: Heritability refers to the proportion of variance in a trait attributable to:
options: Genetic differences among individuals in a population
The total genetic makeup of an individual
quizQuestions.answer: Genetic differences among individuals in a population
quizQuestions.explanation: Heritability is a population-level statistic indicating how much of the variability in a trait is due to genetic differences.

quizQuestions.question: Monozygotic twins share approximately what percentage of their DNA?
quizQuestions.explanation: Monozygotic (identical) twins develop from one fertilized egg and share virtually 100% of their DNA.

quizQuestions.question: Adoption studies test genetic influences on behavior by:
options: Comparing adopted children to their biological vs. adoptive parents
quizQuestions.answer: Comparing adopted children to their biological vs. adoptive parents
quizQuestions.explanation: If adoptees resemble biological parents more than adoptive parents, it suggests genetic influence on that trait.

quizQuestions.question: A heritability coefficient of 0.70 for intelligence means:
options: 70% of people are intelligent due to genes
70% of the population variation in intelligence is attributable to genetic differences
A person's IQ is 70% determined by genes
quizQuestions.answer: 70% of the population variation in intelligence is attributable to genetic differences
quizQuestions.explanation: Heritability applies to populations, not individuals — 0.70 means 70% of IQ variability in that population is explained by genetic differences.

quizQuestions.question: Epigenetics demonstrates that gene expression can be altered by:
options: Environmental experiences without changing the DNA sequence
quizQuestions.answer: Environmental experiences without changing the DNA sequence
quizQuestions.explanation: Epigenetic mechanisms (methylation, histone modification) change gene expression in response to environment without altering DNA.

quizQuestions.question: In Pavlov's original experiments, saliva was the:
quizQuestions.answer: Unconditioned response
quizQuestions.explanation: Salivation to food (UCS) was the natural, unlearned unconditioned response before any conditioning.

quizQuestions.question: Stimulus generalization in classical conditioning occurs when:
options: The original CS no longer produces a CR
Similar stimuli to the CS also elicit the CR
The organism distinguishes between two similar stimuli
The response increases with repeated pairings
quizQuestions.answer: Similar stimuli to the CS also elicit the CR
quizQuestions.explanation: Generalization is the tendency to respond to stimuli similar to the original CS with the same CR.

quizQuestions.question: Taste aversion is a special case of classical conditioning because it:
options: Can be acquired in a single trial with a long delay between CS and UCS
quizQuestions.answer: Can be acquired in a single trial with a long delay between CS and UCS
quizQuestions.explanation: Taste aversions violate typical conditioning rules by forming in a single trial even with hours between CS (food) and UCS (illness).

quizQuestions.question: Counterconditioning, used to treat phobias, involves:
options: Flooding the person with feared stimuli
Pairing the feared stimulus with a positive response to replace the fear
quizQuestions.answer: Pairing the feared stimulus with a positive response to replace the fear
quizQuestions.explanation: Counterconditioning (as in systematic desensitization) replaces a conditioned fear response by associating the CS with relaxation.

quizQuestions.question: Higher-order conditioning occurs when:
options: The UCS is presented more than once
A previously established CS is used to condition a new CS
quizQuestions.answer: A previously established CS is used to condition a new CS
quizQuestions.explanation: In higher-order conditioning, a well-established CS serves as a UCS to condition a new neutral stimulus.

quizQuestions.question: Positive punishment involves:
options: Adding a pleasant stimulus to increase behavior
Removing an unpleasant stimulus to increase behavior
Adding an unpleasant stimulus to decrease behavior
Removing a pleasant stimulus to decrease behavior
quizQuestions.answer: Adding an unpleasant stimulus to decrease behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: Positive punishment adds an aversive stimulus following a behavior, decreasing the likelihood of its recurrence.

quizQuestions.question: Shaping uses:
options: Successive approximations to reinforce behaviors closer to the goal
quizQuestions.answer: Successive approximations to reinforce behaviors closer to the goal
quizQuestions.explanation: Shaping reinforces progressively closer approximations to the target behavior until the full behavior is acquired.

quizQuestions.question: A fixed-interval reinforcement schedule produces a:
options: Pause after reinforcement followed by increasing rate near the next interval
Very high constant rate resistant to extinction
quizQuestions.answer: Pause after reinforcement followed by increasing rate near the next interval
quizQuestions.explanation: Fixed-interval schedules produce the 'scallop pattern' — slow responding after reward, accelerating near the next scheduled reinforcement.

quizQuestions.question: Skinner's radical behaviorism held that:
options: Mental events are the primary cause of behavior
All behavior is determined by its environmental consequences
quizQuestions.answer: All behavior is determined by its environmental consequences
quizQuestions.explanation: Skinner argued behavior is fully explained by reinforcement history; he rejected mental states as causal explanations.

quizQuestions.question: Escape conditioning is a type of:
quizQuestions.answer: Negative reinforcement
quizQuestions.explanation: Escape conditioning reinforces behaviors that terminate an ongoing aversive stimulus — a form of negative reinforcement.

quizQuestions.question: Cognitive distortions, as identified in CBT, are:
options: Habitual irrational thought patterns that contribute to negative emotions
quizQuestions.answer: Habitual irrational thought patterns that contribute to negative emotions
quizQuestions.explanation: Cognitive distortions (e.g., catastrophizing, all-or-nothing thinking) are inaccurate thoughts that maintain psychological distress.

quizQuestions.question: In Ellis's ABC model, 'B' refers to:
options: Belief or self-talk about the event
quizQuestions.answer: Belief or self-talk about the event
quizQuestions.explanation: In REBT's ABC model: A = activating event, B = belief/interpretation, C = emotional and behavioral consequence.

quizQuestions.question: Cognitive restructuring in CBT involves:
options: Identifying and challenging maladaptive thought patterns
Exposure to feared stimuli without cognitive work
quizQuestions.answer: Identifying and challenging maladaptive thought patterns
quizQuestions.explanation: Cognitive restructuring teaches clients to identify cognitive distortions and replace them with more balanced, realistic thoughts.

quizQuestions.question: Aaron Beck's cognitive model of depression proposes that depression is maintained by the cognitive triad, which includes negative views of:
options: The self, the world, and the future
quizQuestions.answer: The self, the world, and the future
quizQuestions.explanation: Beck's cognitive triad identifies three negative thought patterns in depression: pessimistic views of self, the world, and the future.

quizQuestions.question: Metacognition refers to:
options: Thinking about one's own thinking processes
quizQuestions.answer: Thinking about one's own thinking processes
quizQuestions.explanation: Metacognition is awareness and understanding of one's own cognitive processes — 'thinking about thinking.'

quizQuestions.question: Vicarious reinforcement occurs when:
options: A person is directly rewarded for a behavior
A person increases a behavior after observing someone else being rewarded for it
quizQuestions.answer: A person increases a behavior after observing someone else being rewarded for it
quizQuestions.explanation: Vicarious reinforcement — seeing a model rewarded — increases the observer's likelihood of performing the same behavior.

quizQuestions.question: Bandura's Bobo doll experiment demonstrated that children:
options: Will not imitate aggression they observe
Imitate aggressive behaviors they observe in adults
Only imitate behaviors that are directly reinforced
Become less aggressive after watching violent models
quizQuestions.answer: Imitate aggressive behaviors they observe in adults
quizQuestions.explanation: Bandura's study showed children who watched an adult hit a Bobo doll spontaneously imitated the aggressive behaviors.

quizQuestions.question: Self-efficacy beliefs most directly affect:
options: The choice of tasks and persistence in the face of difficulty
quizQuestions.answer: The choice of tasks and persistence in the face of difficulty
quizQuestions.explanation: High self-efficacy leads people to choose challenging tasks and persist longer when facing obstacles.

quizQuestions.question: Reciprocal determinism (Bandura) proposes that behavior is influenced by:
options: The mutual interaction of behavior, personal factors, and environment
quizQuestions.answer: The mutual interaction of behavior, personal factors, and environment
quizQuestions.explanation: Reciprocal determinism holds that behavior, personal characteristics (cognitions), and environment mutually influence each other.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of vicarious punishment?
options: Avoiding speeding after watching someone else receive a speeding ticket
Being told verbally not to speed
quizQuestions.answer: Avoiding speeding after watching someone else receive a speeding ticket
quizQuestions.explanation: Vicarious punishment reduces behavior by observing consequences (punishment) experienced by another person.

quizQuestions.question: A token economy is based on which principle of operant conditioning?
quizQuestions.answer: Generalized conditioned reinforcement
quizQuestions.explanation: Tokens are generalized conditioned reinforcers — neutral stimuli paired with many backup reinforcers — making them broadly motivating.

quizQuestions.question: A functional behavior assessment (FBA) is used to identify:
options: The intelligence level of a student
The function or purpose that a problem behavior serves for the individual
quizQuestions.answer: The function or purpose that a problem behavior serves for the individual
quizQuestions.explanation: FBA determines what environmental triggers and consequences maintain a behavior, informing individualized intervention.

quizQuestions.question: Differential reinforcement of alternative behavior (DRA) involves:
options: Punishing problem behavior and ignoring alternative behavior
Reinforcing an alternative appropriate behavior while withholding reinforcement for problem behavior
Using shaping to increase problem behavior frequency
quizQuestions.answer: Reinforcing an alternative appropriate behavior while withholding reinforcement for problem behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: DRA replaces problem behavior by reinforcing a more acceptable behavior that serves the same function.

quizQuestions.question: Self-monitoring as a behavior modification strategy involves:
options: An individual recording their own target behavior frequency
quizQuestions.answer: An individual recording their own target behavior frequency
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-monitoring builds awareness by having individuals track their own target behavior, which itself often produces behavior change (reactivity).

quizQuestions.question: Extinction in operant conditioning involves:
options: Discontinuing reinforcement so the behavior decreases
Gradual shaping toward a goal behavior
quizQuestions.answer: Discontinuing reinforcement so the behavior decreases
quizQuestions.explanation: Operant extinction occurs when reinforcement is withheld, leading to a gradual decrease and eventual cessation of the behavior.

quizQuestions.question: Loss aversion, a key finding of behavioral economics, means people:
options: Are indifferent to gains and losses of equal amounts
Feel the pain of losses more strongly than the pleasure of equivalent gains
Are more motivated by potential gains than by avoiding losses
quizQuestions.answer: Feel the pain of losses more strongly than the pleasure of equivalent gains
quizQuestions.explanation: Kahneman & Tversky found losses feel roughly twice as powerful as equivalent gains, leading to risk-averse behavior.

quizQuestions.question: A 'nudge' in behavioral economics is:
options: A financial penalty for undesirable behavior
A small choice architecture change that steers behavior without restricting freedom
A legal mandate requiring a specific behavior
quizQuestions.answer: A small choice architecture change that steers behavior without restricting freedom
quizQuestions.explanation: Nudges (Thaler & Sunstein) use default settings and framing to predictably influence choices while preserving freedom of choice.

quizQuestions.question: The anchoring effect demonstrates that people's estimates are heavily influenced by:
options: An initial piece of information presented first
Mood at the time of judgment
quizQuestions.answer: An initial piece of information presented first
quizQuestions.explanation: Anchoring shows that an initial number (even irrelevant) biases subsequent numerical judgments toward that anchor.

quizQuestions.question: Status quo bias in decision-making refers to the tendency to:
options: Always choose the most expensive option
Prefer the current state of affairs over change
Overestimate the probability of rare events
quizQuestions.answer: Prefer the current state of affairs over change
quizQuestions.explanation: Status quo bias reflects loss aversion and inertia — people prefer doing nothing or maintaining the current default.

quizQuestions.question: Prospect theory (Kahneman & Tversky) predicts that people evaluate outcomes:
options: In absolute terms relative to zero
Relative to a reference point such as the current state
quizQuestions.answer: Relative to a reference point such as the current state
quizQuestions.explanation: Prospect theory shows that people evaluate gains and losses relative to a reference point, not in absolute terms.

quizQuestions.question: Herzberg's two-factor theory distinguishes between 'hygiene factors' that prevent dissatisfaction and 'motivators' that:
options: Actively produce job satisfaction and engagement
quizQuestions.answer: Actively produce job satisfaction and engagement
quizQuestions.explanation: Motivators (achievement, recognition, growth) produce positive satisfaction; hygiene factors (salary, safety) only prevent dissatisfaction.

quizQuestions.question: Groupthink in organizational settings refers to:
options: Excessive agreement-seeking that suppresses critical thinking
quizQuestions.answer: Excessive agreement-seeking that suppresses critical thinking
quizQuestions.explanation: Groupthink occurs when the desire for group cohesion overrides realistic appraisal of alternatives, leading to poor decisions.

quizQuestions.question: Transformational leadership is characterized by:
options: Inspiring followers through vision, motivation, and individual consideration
quizQuestions.answer: Inspiring followers through vision, motivation, and individual consideration
quizQuestions.explanation: Transformational leaders inspire followers by articulating vision, modeling values, and attending to individual development.

quizQuestions.question: Organizational culture refers to:
options: The shared values, norms, and assumptions that guide behavior in an organization
quizQuestions.answer: The shared values, norms, and assumptions that guide behavior in an organization
quizQuestions.explanation: Organizational culture comprises the unwritten shared beliefs, values, and behavioral norms that shape how members act.

quizQuestions.question: The Hawthorne Effect refers to the finding that workers' productivity increased because:
options: They knew they were being observed and felt noticed
They were given shorter work hours
quizQuestions.answer: They knew they were being observed and felt noticed
quizQuestions.explanation: The Hawthorne studies found that being watched and given attention — not physical conditions — increased worker output.

quizQuestions.question: The scarcity principle in consumer psychology suggests that products perceived as rare are:
quizQuestions.answer: More desirable
quizQuestions.explanation: Scarcity increases perceived value — limited availability signals desirability and triggers reactance to potential loss.

quizQuestions.question: Social proof as a persuasion technique involves:
options: Demonstrating that many people already use or approve of a product
quizQuestions.answer: Demonstrating that many people already use or approve of a product
quizQuestions.explanation: Social proof leverages the conformity principle — people follow others' behavior as a guide, especially under uncertainty.

quizQuestions.question: The mere exposure effect in consumer behavior demonstrates that:
options: Advertising must be novel to be effective
Repeated exposure to a product increases liking of it
quizQuestions.answer: Repeated exposure to a product increases liking of it
quizQuestions.explanation: Zajonc's mere exposure effect shows familiarity breeds liking — repeated encounters increase positive evaluation.

quizQuestions.question: Brand loyalty is most strongly associated with which psychological concept?
options: Classical conditioning, identity, and cognitive consistency
quizQuestions.answer: Classical conditioning, identity, and cognitive consistency
quizQuestions.explanation: Brand loyalty is sustained by positive associations (classical conditioning), brand-self identification, and consistency with past choices.

quizQuestions.question: Impulse buying is most strongly predicted by:
options: Emotional state and reduced self-regulatory control
quizQuestions.answer: Emotional state and reduced self-regulatory control
quizQuestions.explanation: Impulse purchases are driven by emotional arousal, positive mood, and reduced capacity for self-regulation.

quizQuestions.question: Environmental psychology research has found that exposure to natural environments tends to:
quizQuestions.answer: Restore attention and reduce stress
quizQuestions.explanation: Attention Restoration Theory (Kaplan) and stress recovery research show nature contact restores directed attention and reduces cortisol.

quizQuestions.question: High residential density (crowding) is most consistently associated with:
options: Increased stress, reduced privacy, and lower sense of control
quizQuestions.answer: Increased stress, reduced privacy, and lower sense of control
quizQuestions.explanation: Research on crowding links high density to increased physiological stress, reduced privacy, and perceived loss of control.

quizQuestions.question: The broken windows theory proposes that:
options: Physical crime environments cause psychological disorders
Signs of disorder and neglect in an environment invite more disorder
People are naturally law-abiding regardless of environment
Urban environments are inherently more stressful
quizQuestions.answer: Signs of disorder and neglect in an environment invite more disorder
quizQuestions.explanation: Broken windows theory holds that visible signs of neglect (broken windows, graffiti) signal that deviance is tolerated, encouraging further disorder.

quizQuestions.question: Noise pollution research shows that chronic exposure to high noise levels most consistently impairs:
quizQuestions.answer: Reading, memory, and stress regulation
quizQuestions.explanation: Chronic noise exposure has been linked to reading deficits in children, impaired memory, and elevated cortisol levels.

quizQuestions.question: Personal space, as studied by Sommer and Hall, refers to:
options: The invisible buffer zone people maintain around themselves in social interactions
The workspace in an office setting
The distance required for effective communication
quizQuestions.answer: The invisible buffer zone people maintain around themselves in social interactions
quizQuestions.explanation: Personal space is the culturally and individually variable zone of space people prefer to maintain during social interactions.

quizQuestions.question: The Transtheoretical Model's 'contemplation' stage describes someone who:
options: Has no intention of changing behavior
Is aware of a problem and thinking about change within 6 months
Has already changed behavior for 6 months
Is actively changing behavior right now
quizQuestions.answer: Is aware of a problem and thinking about change within 6 months
quizQuestions.explanation: In the TTM, contemplators recognize a problem and intend to change within 6 months but have not yet committed to action.

quizQuestions.question: The Health Belief Model proposes that people take health action when they believe:
options: The threat is severe, they are susceptible, benefits outweigh barriers, and a cue triggers action
quizQuestions.answer: The threat is severe, they are susceptible, benefits outweigh barriers, and a cue triggers action
quizQuestions.explanation: The HBM identifies perceived severity, susceptibility, benefits, barriers, and cues to action as determinants of health behavior.

quizQuestions.question: Health behavior change interventions based on motivational interviewing aim to:
options: Confront and challenge the client directly
Elicit the client's own motivation and resolve ambivalence toward change
Prescribe specific behaviors for the client to follow
Use token economies to reward healthy behavior
quizQuestions.answer: Elicit the client's own motivation and resolve ambivalence toward change
quizQuestions.explanation: Motivational interviewing is a collaborative, client-centered approach that evokes internal motivation by exploring ambivalence.

quizQuestions.question: Social norms marketing in public health campaigns works by:
options: Shaming people who engage in unhealthy behavior
Correcting misperceptions about how common healthy or unhealthy behaviors actually are
Providing financial incentives for healthy behavior
quizQuestions.answer: Correcting misperceptions about how common healthy or unhealthy behaviors actually are
quizQuestions.explanation: Social norms approaches correct overestimates of unhealthy peer behavior (e.g., alcohol use), using accurate norms to reduce target behavior.

quizQuestions.question: The biopsychosocial model of health holds that illness is best explained by:
options: The interaction of biological, psychological, and social factors
quizQuestions.answer: The interaction of biological, psychological, and social factors
quizQuestions.explanation: The biopsychosocial model integrates genetic/biological, psychological, and social/environmental factors in understanding health and illness.

quizQuestions.question: Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA) is most grounded in which psychological tradition?
quizQuestions.answer: Operant conditioning and radical behaviorism
quizQuestions.explanation: ABA applies Skinnerian operant conditioning principles systematically to measure and modify socially significant behavior.

quizQuestions.question: Discrete Trial Training (DTT) in ABA consists of:
options: Structured individual trials with a prompt, response, and reinforcement
quizQuestions.answer: Structured individual trials with a prompt, response, and reinforcement
quizQuestions.explanation: DTT breaks skills into small components taught through structured trials with a stimulus, response opportunity, and immediate reinforcement.

quizQuestions.question: A common ethical criticism of ABA historically involves:
options: The use of aversive or punitive procedures and its impact on dignity
quizQuestions.answer: The use of aversive or punitive procedures and its impact on dignity
quizQuestions.explanation: Historically, some ABA programs used electric shocks and other aversives, raising serious ethical concerns about dignity and autonomy.

quizQuestions.question: Generalization in ABA therapy means that learned skills:
options: Are performed only in the training setting
Transfer to new settings, people, and materials beyond the training context
quizQuestions.answer: Transfer to new settings, people, and materials beyond the training context
quizQuestions.explanation: Generalization is a key goal of ABA — skills must transfer from training conditions to natural real-world contexts to be functional.

quizQuestions.question: A Board Certified Behavior Analyst (BCBA) is a professional who:
options: Has an M.D. specializing in psychiatry
Has met certification standards for designing and supervising ABA programs
Is licensed to prescribe behavior-modifying medications
Has a degree in social work
quizQuestions.answer: Has met certification standards for designing and supervising ABA programs
quizQuestions.explanation: BCBAs hold master's-level or higher credentials and pass a certification exam to design and oversee ABA interventions.

questions.question: Which field is considered the foundation of modern behavioral science?
questions.answer: Psychology
questions.explanation: Psychology is the primary disciplinary foundation of behavioral science, providing methods, theories, and empirical findings.

questions.question: Kin selection theory explains altruistic behavior toward relatives as driven by:
options: Shared genetic interest — helping relatives propagates shared genes
questions.answer: Shared genetic interest — helping relatives propagates shared genes
questions.explanation: Hamilton's rule: altruism toward kin is favored by natural selection because it increases inclusive fitness through shared genes.

questions.question: Twin studies showing identical twins raised apart are more similar than fraternal twins raised together provide evidence for:
options: The influence of shared family environment
Strong genetic contributions to trait variance
questions.answer: Strong genetic contributions to trait variance
questions.explanation: MZA twins sharing genetics but not environment being similar suggests genetic factors drive variance in those traits.

questions.question: In classical conditioning, extinction occurs when:
options: The CS is repeatedly presented without the UCS
The UCS is presented alone many times
The UCR is strengthened by repetition
questions.answer: The CS is repeatedly presented without the UCS
questions.explanation: Extinction weakens the CR by repeatedly presenting the CS in the absence of the UCS, breaking the association.

questions.question: Which reinforcement schedule produces responding most resistant to extinction?
questions.answer: Variable-ratio
questions.explanation: Variable-ratio schedules (e.g., slot machines) produce the highest rates of responding and the greatest resistance to extinction.

questions.question: Beck's cognitive triad in depression consists of negative views of:
options: The past, the present, and other people
The self, the world, and the future
questions.answer: The self, the world, and the future
questions.explanation: Beck identified three domains of negative automatic thinking in depression: self (worthless), world (unfair), and future (hopeless).

questions.question: Vicarious reinforcement in social learning theory refers to:
options: Learning by watching others receive rewards
questions.answer: Learning by watching others receive rewards
questions.explanation: Vicarious reinforcement increases the observer's likelihood of performing the behavior because they see the model rewarded.

questions.question: A token economy is most effective when tokens are:
options: Backed by meaningful reinforcers (backup reinforcers) and given contingently
Withheld until a week of good behavior
questions.answer: Backed by meaningful reinforcers (backup reinforcers) and given contingently
questions.explanation: Tokens gain reinforcing value from the backup reinforcers they can be exchanged for; contingent delivery maintains behavior change.

questions.question: Loss aversion is best demonstrated by which scenario?
options: People invest equally in gains and losses
People work harder to avoid losing $50 than to gain $50
People prefer safe options in all decisions
People ignore sunk costs in decision-making
questions.answer: People work harder to avoid losing $50 than to gain $50
questions.explanation: Loss aversion means the psychological impact of a loss is greater than an equal gain, motivating stronger effort to avoid losses.

questions.question: The Hawthorne Effect warns researchers that:
options: Participants may change behavior when they know they are being observed
Laboratory studies have no external validity
questions.answer: Participants may change behavior when they know they are being observed
questions.explanation: The Hawthorne Effect is a reactivity phenomenon — being observed changes behavior, threatening research validity.

questions.question: Social proof influences consumers primarily through:
options: Fear of missing out on a sale
The conformity tendency to use others' behavior as a guide
questions.answer: The conformity tendency to use others' behavior as a guide
questions.explanation: Social proof (Cialdini) leverages normative influence — people assume popular choices are correct choices.

questions.question: Attention Restoration Theory predicts that natural environments improve performance because they:
options: Allow directed attention to recover through involuntary fascination
questions.answer: Allow directed attention to recover through involuntary fascination
questions.explanation: Kaplan's ART holds that nature engages effortless involuntary attention, allowing the directed attention system to rest and recover.

questions.question: The precontemplation stage in the Transtheoretical Model describes someone who:
options: Has no intention of changing in the next 6 months
questions.answer: Has no intention of changing in the next 6 months
questions.explanation: Precontemplators may not recognize the behavior as a problem or may feel unable or unwilling to change.

questions.question: Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA) focuses on:
options: Systematically changing socially significant behavior using operant principles
questions.answer: Systematically changing socially significant behavior using operant principles
questions.explanation: ABA applies behavioral science systematically to improve behaviors that matter for quality of life using reinforcement-based methods.

questions.question: Heritability coefficients can only be interpreted in the context of:
options: A specific population in a specific environment
questions.answer: A specific population in a specific environment
questions.explanation: Heritability is a population statistic that varies depending on the genetic and environmental variability present in that sample.

questions.question: An evolutionary explanation for jealousy in romantic relationships proposes that it evolved to:
options: Protect paternity certainty and mate investment
questions.answer: Protect paternity certainty and mate investment
questions.explanation: Evolutionary theory holds that jealousy evolved as a mate-retention strategy to protect investment in offspring and paternity certainty.

questions.question: Gene-environment interaction (GxE) refers to:
options: Genes determining behavior independently of environment
The same genotype producing different phenotypes in different environments
Gene expression remaining fixed across all environments
questions.answer: The same genotype producing different phenotypes in different environments
questions.explanation: GxE interaction occurs when the effect of a genotype on behavior depends on the environment in which the organism develops.

questions.question: Preparedness theory (Seligman) in classical conditioning proposes that organisms are biologically predisposed to:
options: Learn some associations (especially survival-relevant ones) more readily than others
questions.answer: Learn some associations (especially survival-relevant ones) more readily than others
questions.explanation: Preparedness theory holds that evolution has predisposed organisms to associate stimuli that were threats or resources for survival.

questions.question: Negative punishment (response cost) involves:
options: Adding an unpleasant event to decrease behavior
Removing a pleasant stimulus to decrease behavior
Removing an unpleasant stimulus to increase behavior
Adding a pleasant stimulus to increase behavior
questions.answer: Removing a pleasant stimulus to decrease behavior
questions.explanation: Negative punishment removes something valued (privileges, tokens) contingent on the unwanted behavior, decreasing it.

questions.question: Cognitive behavioral approaches differ from pure behaviorism by emphasizing:
options: The mediating role of thoughts and beliefs in behavior
questions.answer: The mediating role of thoughts and beliefs in behavior
questions.explanation: CBT integrates cognition into behavioral theory — thoughts and beliefs mediate between stimuli/events and behavioral/emotional responses.

questions.question: Bandura's concept of self-regulation involves:
options: The capacity to set standards, monitor behavior, and adjust it toward goals
questions.answer: The capacity to set standards, monitor behavior, and adjust it toward goals
questions.explanation: Self-regulation is the internally driven process of goal setting, self-monitoring, self-evaluation, and self-reinforcement.

questions.question: Which principle explains why organ donation rates are much higher in countries that use an opt-out (default donor) policy?
options: Default effect and status quo bias
questions.answer: Default effect and status quo bias
questions.explanation: The default effect shows people tend to keep the default option; opt-out policies make donation the default, dramatically increasing rates.

questions.question: Groupthink is most likely to occur in a group that:
options: Is highly cohesive and led by a directive leader
Has diverse membership with different perspectives
questions.answer: Is highly cohesive and led by a directive leader
questions.explanation: Groupthink flourishes in cohesive, insulated groups with directive leadership where dissent is implicitly discouraged.

questions.question: The reciprocity principle in consumer psychology means that:
options: People feel obligated to return a favor or gift
questions.answer: People feel obligated to return a favor or gift
questions.explanation: Cialdini's reciprocity principle holds that receiving something (free sample, favor) creates a felt obligation to give back.

questions.question: Research consistently shows that which environmental factor most strongly predicts urban crime rates?
questions.answer: Concentrated poverty and social disorganization
questions.explanation: Social disorganization theory links crime to neighborhood characteristics like poverty, residential instability, and weak social ties.

questions.question: Which stage of the Transtheoretical Model involves actively practicing a new behavior that began within the past 6 months?
questions.answer: Action
questions.explanation: The action stage begins when the person has started making observable behavior changes, defined as within the past 6 months.

questions.question: Maintenance of behavior change in ABA requires strategies like:
options: Thinning reinforcement schedules and building natural community reinforcement
questions.answer: Thinning reinforcement schedules and building natural community reinforcement
questions.explanation: For maintenance, ABA practitioners thin reinforcement gradually and program behavior to contact naturally occurring reinforcers in the environment.

questions.question: Reciprocal determinism (Bandura) differs from strict behaviorism by recognizing that:
options: Cognitive factors influence how the environment affects behavior
questions.answer: Cognitive factors influence how the environment affects behavior
questions.explanation: Reciprocal determinism holds that cognition, behavior, and environment mutually interact — cognition is not just a product of environment.

questions.question: The Health Belief Model component 'perceived barriers' refers to:
options: Obstacles (cost, time, side effects) that discourage taking health action
The individual's belief in their susceptibility
questions.answer: Obstacles (cost, time, side effects) that discourage taking health action
questions.explanation: Perceived barriers are the individual's assessment of the obstacles (practical, emotional) that impede taking the recommended health action.

questions.question: A behavior analyst conducting an FBA observes that a student's tantrums result in being sent to the hallway. This suggests the behavior is maintained by:
options: Escape or avoidance of task demands
questions.answer: Escape or avoidance of task demands
questions.explanation: If tantrums result in leaving the classroom, the behavior is negatively reinforced by escape from aversive academic demands.

questions.question: Prospect theory suggests that people are risk-averse when considering gains and risk-seeking when:
questions.answer: Facing certain losses
questions.explanation: Kahneman & Tversky found people prefer a certain gain over a gamble (risk-averse) but gamble to avoid a certain loss (risk-seeking).

questions.question: Environmental enrichment studies in animals and humans suggest that complex, stimulating environments:
options: Promote brain development and cognitive performance
questions.answer: Promote brain development and cognitive performance
questions.explanation: Environmental enrichment promotes neuroplasticity, dendritic growth, and improved learning and memory performance in both animals and humans.

questions.question: Self-efficacy for a health behavior is most effectively increased by:
options: Watching others fail at the behavior
questions.answer: Mastery experiences (successfully performing the behavior)
questions.explanation: Bandura identified mastery experiences as the most powerful source of self-efficacy — nothing builds confidence like actual success.

questions.question: Sensory reinforcement in ABA refers to behavior maintained by:
options: Automatic sensory feedback produced by the behavior itself
questions.answer: Automatic sensory feedback produced by the behavior itself
questions.explanation: Automatically reinforced behaviors produce sensory consequences (e.g., rocking, hand-flapping) that are intrinsically reinforcing.

questions.question: Behavioral economics has most directly influenced which public policy area?
options: Retirement savings, healthcare choices, and environmental behavior
questions.answer: Retirement savings, healthcare choices, and environmental behavior
questions.explanation: Nudge units in governments worldwide apply behavioral economics to improve retirement savings rates, organ donation, and sustainability behaviors.
